From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Administrative divisions of New York City

"Five boroughs" redirects here. For the English boroughs controlled for a period by the Vikings, see [Five Boroughs of the Danelaw](/wiki/Five_Boroughs_of_the_Danelaw "Five Boroughs of the Danelaw").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/5_Boroughs_Labels_New_York_City_Map.svg/330px-5_Boroughs_Labels_New_York_City_Map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:5_Boroughs_Labels_New_York_City_Map.svg)

1. [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") (New York County)

2. [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") (Kings County)

3. [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens") (Queens County)

4. [The Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx") (Bronx County)

5. [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island") (Richmond County)

Note: [JFK](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_International_Airport "John F. Kennedy International Airport") and [LGA](/wiki/LaGuardia_Airport "LaGuardia Airport") airports, marked in brown, are located in Queens.

The **boroughs of New York City** are the five major governmental [districts](/wiki/District "District") that compose [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"). They are [the Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx"), [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), and [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island"). Each borough is coextensive with a respective [county](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_New_York_(state)#County "Administrative divisions of New York (state)") of the [State of New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"): The Bronx is Bronx County, Brooklyn is Kings County, Manhattan is New York County, Queens is Queens County, and Staten Island is Richmond County.

All five boroughs of New York came into existence with the creation of [modern New York City](/wiki/City_of_Greater_New_York "City of Greater New York") in 1898, when New York County (then including the Bronx), Kings County, Richmond County, and part of Queens County were consolidated within one municipal government under a [new city charter](/wiki/New_York_City_Charter "New York City Charter"). All former municipalities within the newly consolidated city were dissolved.

New York City was originally confined to Manhattan Island and the smaller surrounding islands that formed New York County. As the city grew northward, it began annexing areas on the mainland, absorbing territory from [Westchester County](/wiki/Westchester_County,_New_York "Westchester County, New York") into New York County in 1874 ([West Bronx](/wiki/West_Bronx "West Bronx")) and 1895 ([East Bronx](/wiki/East_Bronx "East Bronx")). During the 1898 consolidation, this territory was organized as the Borough of the Bronx, though still part of New York County. In 1914, Bronx County was split off from New York County so that each borough was then coterminous with a county.

When the western part of Queens County was consolidated with New York City in 1898, that area became the Borough of Queens. In 1899, the remaining eastern section of Queens County was split off to form [Nassau County](/wiki/Nassau_County,_New_York "Nassau County, New York") on [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), thereafter making the borough and county of Queens coextensive with each other.

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Boroughs_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

The term *borough* was adopted in 1898 to describe a form of [governmental administration](/wiki/Administration_(government) "Administration (government)") for each of the five fundamental constituent parts of the newly [consolidated city](/wiki/History_of_New_York_City_(1898%E2%80%931945) "History of New York City (1898–1945)"). Under the 1898 City Charter adopted by the New York State Legislature, a borough is a [municipal corporation](/wiki/Municipal_corporation "Municipal corporation") that is created when a county is merged with populated areas within it.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The limited powers of the boroughs are inferior to the authority of the [government of New York City](/wiki/Government_of_New_York_City "Government of New York City"). This contrasts with the powers of boroughs in [Connecticut](/wiki/Borough_(Connecticut) "Borough (Connecticut)"), [New Jersey](/wiki/Borough_(New_Jersey) "Borough (New Jersey)"), and [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Borough_(Pennsylvania) "Borough (Pennsylvania)"), where a borough is an independent level of government; as well as with borough forms used in [other states](/wiki/Borough_(United_States) "Borough (United States)"), in [Greater London](/wiki/London_boroughs "London boroughs"), and [boroughs in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Borough_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "Borough status in the United Kingdom").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Boroughs_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Background")]

New York City is often referred to collectively as the **five boroughs**, which can unambiguously refer to the city proper as a whole, avoiding confusion with any particular borough or with the [Greater New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area"). The term is also used by politicians to counter a frequent focus on Manhattan and thereby to place all five boroughs on equal footing. In the same vein, the term *outer boroughs* refers to all of the boroughs excluding Manhattan, even though the geographic center of the city is along the Brooklyn–Queens border.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/5_boros_of_NYC_in_1814.jpg/250px-5_boros_of_NYC_in_1814.jpg)](/wiki/File:5_boros_of_NYC_in_1814.jpg)

The current five boroughs of Greater New York as they appeared in 1814. The Bronx was part of Westchester County. Queens County included modern Nassau County. Kings County had six towns, one of which was Brooklyn. New York City is shown in lower Manhattan.

### Changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Boroughs_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Changes")]

All five boroughs were created in 1898 during consolidation, when the city's modern boundaries were established.

The Bronx originally included parts of New York County outside of Manhattan that had previously been ceded by neighboring [Westchester County](/wiki/Westchester_County,_New_York "Westchester County, New York") in two stages; in 1874 ([southern Yonkers](/wiki/Riverdale,_Bronx "Riverdale, Bronx"), and the towns of [Kingsbridge](/wiki/Kingsbridge,_Bronx "Kingsbridge, Bronx"), [West Farms](/wiki/West_Farms,_Bronx "West Farms, Bronx"), and [Morrisania](/wiki/Morrisania,_Bronx "Morrisania, Bronx")) and then following a [referendum](/wiki/Referendum "Referendum") in 1894 (towns of [Westchester](/wiki/Westchester_Square,_Bronx "Westchester Square, Bronx"), [Williamsbridge](/wiki/Williamsbridge,_Bronx "Williamsbridge, Bronx"), and the southern portion of [Eastchester](/wiki/Eastchester,_New_York "Eastchester, New York")).[[7]](#cite_note-7) In 1914, the present-day separate Bronx County became the most recent county to be created in the State of New York.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The borough of Queens consists of what formerly was only the western part of a then-larger Queens County. In 1899, the three eastern towns of Queens County that had not joined the city the year before—the towns of [Hempstead](/wiki/Hempstead,_New_York "Hempstead, New York"), [North Hempstead](/wiki/North_Hempstead,_New_York "North Hempstead, New York"), and [Oyster Bay](/wiki/Oyster_Bay_(town),_New_York "Oyster Bay (town), New York")—formally seceded from Queens County to form the new [Nassau County](/wiki/Nassau_County,_New_York "Nassau County, New York").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The borough of Staten Island, coextensive with Richmond County, was officially the borough of Richmond until the name was changed in 1975 to reflect its common appellation, while leaving the name of the county unchanged.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Description of the boroughs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Boroughs_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Description of the boroughs")]

Further information: [Neighborhoods in New York City](/wiki/Neighborhoods_in_New_York_City "Neighborhoods in New York City") and [List of parks in New York City](/wiki/List_of_parks_in_New_York_City "List of parks in New York City")

There are hundreds of distinct neighborhoods throughout the five boroughs of New York City, many with a definable history and character to call their own.

### Manhattan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Boroughs_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Manhattan")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Chinatown_manhattan_2009.JPG/250px-Chinatown_manhattan_2009.JPG)](/wiki/File:Chinatown_manhattan_2009.JPG)

[Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_Manhattan "Chinatown, Manhattan") in Manhattan, the most densely populated borough of New York City, [with a higher density than any individual American city](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population_density#New_York_City_boroughs "List of United States cities by population density")

[Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") (co-extensive with New York County) is the geographically smallest and most densely populated borough. It is the symbol of New York City, as home to most of the city's [skyscrapers](/wiki/Skyscraper "Skyscraper") and prominent landmarks, including [Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square "Times Square") and [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park"); and may be locally known simply as *The City*.[[11]](#cite_note-ManhattanTheCity-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) Manhattan's (New York County's) population density of 72,033 people per square mile (27,812/km2) in 2015 makes it the [highest of any county in the United States](/wiki/County_statistics_of_the_United_States#Population_density "County statistics of the United States") and [higher than the density of any individual U.S. city](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population_density#New_York_City_boroughs "List of United States cities by population density").[[13]](#cite_note-13) Manhattan is the cultural, administrative, and [financial center](/wiki/Financial_center "Financial center") of New York City and contains the [headquarters](/wiki/Headquarters "Headquarters") of many major [multinational corporations](/wiki/Multinational_corporation "Multinational corporation"), the [United Nations Headquarters](/wiki/United_Nations_Headquarters "United Nations Headquarters"), [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street"), and a number of important [universities](/wiki/University "University"). Manhattan is often described by Americans as the cultural, financial, [media](/wiki/Media_(communication) "Media (communication)"), and [entertainment](/wiki/Show_business "Show business") capital of the world.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-ManhattanMediaEntertainmentCapital-18)

Most of the borough is situated on [Manhattan Island](/wiki/Manhattan_Island "Manhattan Island"), at the mouth of the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"). Several small islands are also part of the borough of Manhattan, including [Randall's Island](/wiki/Randall%27s_Island "Randall's Island"), [Wards Island](/wiki/Wards_Island "Wards Island"), and [Roosevelt Island](/wiki/Roosevelt_Island "Roosevelt Island") in the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River"), and [Governors Island](/wiki/Governors_Island "Governors Island") to the south in [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor"); [Liberty Island](/wiki/Liberty_Island "Liberty Island"), on which the [Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty "Statue of Liberty") stands, is a Manhattan [exclave](/wiki/Exclave "Exclave"), as is the original portion of [Ellis Island](/wiki/Ellis_Island "Ellis Island").[[19]](#cite_note-Islands-19)

Manhattan Island is loosely divided into [Lower](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan"), [Midtown](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan"), and [Uptown](/wiki/Upper_Manhattan "Upper Manhattan") regions. Uptown Manhattan is divided by Central Park into the [Upper East Side](/wiki/Upper_East_Side "Upper East Side") and the [Upper West Side](/wiki/Upper_West_Side "Upper West Side"), and above the park is [Harlem](/wiki/Harlem "Harlem"). Marble Hill was part of the northern tip of Manhattan Island, but the [Harlem River Ship Canal](/wiki/Harlem_River_Ship_Canal "Harlem River Ship Canal"), dug in 1895, separated it from the remainder of Manhattan.[[20]](#cite_note-canal-20)

Before World War I, the old channel was filled in and Marble Hill became part of the mainland.[[21]](#cite_note-21) After a May 1984 court ruled that Marble Hill was part of Bronx County (not New York County),[[22]](#cite_note-22) the matter was definitively settled later that year when the [New York Legislature](/wiki/New_York_Legislature "New York Legislature") overwhelmingly passed legislation declaring the neighborhood part of both New York County and the Borough of Manhattan.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

New York City's remaining four boroughs are collectively referred to as the outer boroughs.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Brooklyn

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Boroughs_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Brooklyn")]

[![A view down a street with rowhouses in brown, white, and various shades of red](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Greenpoint_Houses.JPG/250px-Greenpoint_Houses.JPG)](/wiki/File:Greenpoint_Houses.JPG)

Landmark nineteenth-century [brownstones](/wiki/Brownstone "Brownstone") in the [Greenpoint Historic District](/wiki/Greenpoint,_Brooklyn "Greenpoint, Brooklyn") of [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), New York City's most populous borough

[Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") (co-extensive with Kings County), on the western tip of [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), is the city's most populous borough. Brooklyn is known for its cultural, social, and ethnic diversity, an independent art scene, [distinct neighborhoods](/wiki/List_of_Brooklyn_neighborhoods "List of Brooklyn neighborhoods"), and a distinctive architectural heritage. [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn") is the largest central core neighborhood in the outer boroughs. The borough has a long beachfront shoreline including [Coney Island](/wiki/Coney_Island "Coney Island"), established in the 1870s as one of the earliest amusement grounds in the country.[[26]](#cite_note-26) [Marine Park](/wiki/Marine_Park_(Brooklyn_park) "Marine Park (Brooklyn park)")[[27]](#cite_note-LargestParksNYC-27) and [Prospect Park](/wiki/Prospect_Park_(Brooklyn) "Prospect Park (Brooklyn)") are the two largest parks in Brooklyn. Since the early 2010s, Brooklyn has evolved into a thriving hub of [entrepreneurship](/wiki/Entrepreneurship "Entrepreneurship") and [high technology](/wiki/High_tech "High tech") [startup firms](/wiki/Startup_company "Startup company"),[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-BrooklynDesignHub-29) and of [postmodern art](/wiki/Postmodern_art "Postmodern art")[[30]](#cite_note-BrooklynArt1-30) and [design](/wiki/Design "Design").[[29]](#cite_note-BrooklynDesignHub-29)

### Queens

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Boroughs_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Queens")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Unisphere_in_summer.jpg/250px-Unisphere_in_summer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Unisphere_in_summer.jpg)

The [Unisphere](/wiki/Unisphere "Unisphere") in [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), the most [ethnically diverse](/wiki/Ethnic_diversity "Ethnic diversity") urban area in the world[[31]](#cite_note-QueensMostDiverseWorld1-31)[[32]](#cite_note-QueensMostDiverseWorld2-32)

[Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens") (co-extensive with Queens County), on [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island") north and east of Brooklyn, is geographically the largest borough, the most [ethnically diverse](/wiki/Ethnic_diversity "Ethnic diversity") county in the United States,[[33]](#cite_note-queensdiverse-33) as well as the most ethnically diverse urban area in the world.[[31]](#cite_note-QueensMostDiverseWorld1-31)[[32]](#cite_note-QueensMostDiverseWorld2-32) Historically a collection of small towns and villages founded by the [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_people "Dutch people"), the borough has since developed both commercial and residential prominence. [Downtown Flushing](/wiki/Downtown_Flushing "Downtown Flushing") has become one of the busiest central core neighborhoods in the outer boroughs.

Parts of Queens such as [Bellerose](/wiki/Bellerose,_Queens "Bellerose, Queens") and [Forest Hills](/wiki/Forest_Hills,_Queens "Forest Hills, Queens") are relatively suburban in character. Queens is the site of [Citi Field](/wiki/Citi_Field "Citi Field"), the [baseball stadium](/wiki/Baseball_stadium "Baseball stadium") of the [New York Mets](/wiki/New_York_Mets "New York Mets"), and hosts the annual [U.S. Open tennis tournament](/wiki/US_Open_(tennis) "US Open (tennis)") at [Flushing Meadows-Corona Park](/wiki/Flushing_Meadows-Corona_Park "Flushing Meadows-Corona Park"). Additionally, two of the three busiest airports serving the New York metropolitan area, [John F. Kennedy International Airport](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_International_Airport "John F. Kennedy International Airport") and [LaGuardia Airport](/wiki/LaGuardia_Airport "LaGuardia Airport"), are located in Queens. The third is [Newark Liberty International Airport](/wiki/Newark_Liberty_International_Airport "Newark Liberty International Airport") in [Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"), [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey").

### The Bronx

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Boroughs_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: The Bronx")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Melrosebx1.JPG/250px-Melrosebx1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Melrosebx1.JPG)

[The Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx"), the northernmost borough of New York City and the only borough situated primarily on the United States mainland

[The Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx") (co-extensive with Bronx County) is New York City's northernmost borough and is the only New York City borough that is part of the United States mainland (excluding some islands that are part of the borough). It is the location of [Yankee Stadium](/wiki/Yankee_Stadium "Yankee Stadium"), the baseball stadium of the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees"), and home to [Co-op City](/wiki/Co-op_City,_Bronx "Co-op City, Bronx"), the largest [cooperatively owned housing](/wiki/Housing_cooperative "Housing cooperative") complex in the United States.[[34]](#cite_note-34) It is home to the [Bronx Zoo](/wiki/Bronx_Zoo "Bronx Zoo"), the world's largest metropolitan zoo,[[35]](#cite_note-BronxZoo-35) which covers 265 acres (107 ha) and houses over 6,000 animals.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Directly to the zoo's north is the [New York Botanical Garden](/wiki/New_York_Botanical_Garden "New York Botanical Garden"), a botanical garden and National Historic Landmark. [Pelham Bay Park](/wiki/Pelham_Bay_Park "Pelham Bay Park") is the largest park in New York City, at 2,772 acres (1,122 ha).[[27]](#cite_note-LargestParksNYC-27)

### Staten Island

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Boroughs_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Staten Island")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Staten_Island_Borough_Hall_sign.jpg/250px-Staten_Island_Borough_Hall_sign.jpg)](/wiki/File:Staten_Island_Borough_Hall_sign.jpg)

[Borough Hall](/wiki/Staten_Island_Borough_Hall "Staten Island Borough Hall") in the [St. George](/wiki/St._George,_Staten_Island "St. George, Staten Island") neighborhood of [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island"), the most [suburban](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") borough of New York City

[Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island") (co-extensive with Richmond County) is generally the most [suburban](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") in character of the five boroughs. Staten Island is connected to Brooklyn by the [Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge](/wiki/Verrazzano%E2%80%93Narrows_Bridge "Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge") and to Manhattan by way of the [Staten Island Ferry](/wiki/Staten_Island_Ferry "Staten Island Ferry"), a free [commuter](/wiki/Commuter "Commuter") ferry and popular tourist attraction which provides unobstructed views of the Statue of Liberty, [Ellis Island](/wiki/Ellis_Island "Ellis Island"), and [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan"). In central Staten Island, the [Staten Island Greenbelt](/wiki/Staten_Island_Greenbelt "Staten Island Greenbelt") spans approximately 2,500 acres (10 km2), including 28 miles (45 km) of walking trails and one of the last undisturbed forests in the city.[[37]](#cite_note-37) Designated in 1984 to protect the island's natural lands, the Greenbelt comprises seven city parks.

## Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Boroughs_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Governance")]

Main article: [Borough president](/wiki/Borough_president "Borough president")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/New_York_City_Demographics_01_500px-yellowbrooklyn.png/250px-New_York_City_Demographics_01_500px-yellowbrooklyn.png)](/wiki/File:New_York_City_Demographics_01_500px-yellowbrooklyn.png)

Populations before 1898 are for the areas now enclosed in the present boroughs.

Since 1914, each of New York City's five boroughs has been coextensive with a county of New York State—unlike most [U.S. cities](/wiki/City#United_States "City"), which lie within a single [county](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)") or extend partially into another county, constitute a county in themselves, or are completely separate and [independent](/wiki/Independent_city_(United_States) "Independent city (United States)") of any county.

Each borough is represented by a [borough president](/wiki/Borough_president "Borough president"). Brooklyn, Queens, and Staten Island each have a Borough Hall with limited administrative functions. The Manhattan Borough President's office is situated in the [Manhattan Municipal Building](/wiki/Manhattan_Municipal_Building "Manhattan Municipal Building"). The Bronx Borough President's office used to be in its own [Bronx Borough Hall](/wiki/Bronx_Borough_Hall "Bronx Borough Hall") but has been in the [Bronx County Courthouse](/wiki/Bronx_County_Courthouse "Bronx County Courthouse") for decades. Since the abolition of the [Board of Estimate](/wiki/New_York_City_Board_of_Estimate "New York City Board of Estimate") in 1990, due to a [1989 ruling](/wiki/Board_of_Estimate_of_City_of_New_York_v._Morris "Board of Estimate of City of New York v. Morris") of the [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/United_States_Supreme_Court "United States Supreme Court"),[[38]](#cite_note-38) the borough presidents have minimal executive powers, and there is no legislative function within a borough.

Executive functions in New York City are the responsibility of the [Mayor of New York City](/wiki/Mayor_of_New_York_City "Mayor of New York City"), while legislative functions reside with the [New York City Council](/wiki/New_York_City_Council "New York City Council"). The borough presidents primarily act as spokesmen, advocates, and [ceremonial leaders](/wiki/Figurehead "Figurehead") for their boroughs, have budgets from which they can allocate relatively modest sums of money to community organizations and projects, and appoint the members of the 59 largely advisory [community boards](/wiki/Community_boards_of_New_York_City "Community boards of New York City") in the city's various neighborhoods. The Brooklyn and Queens borough presidents also appoint trustees to the local [public library systems](/wiki/New_York_City#Public_library_system "New York City") in those boroughs.

Being coextensive with an individual county, each borough also elects a [district attorney](/wiki/District_attorney "District attorney"), as does every other county of New York State. While the district attorneys of Manhattan and Brooklyn are popularly referred to as "Manhattan D.A. [Cyrus Vance, Jr.](/wiki/Cyrus_Vance,_Jr. "Cyrus Vance, Jr.")", or "Brooklyn D.A. [Kenneth P. Thompson](/wiki/Kenneth_P._Thompson "Kenneth P. Thompson")" by the media, they are technically and legally the district attorneys of New York County and Kings County, respectively. The same goes for Staten Island. There is no such distinction made for the district attorneys of the other two counties, Queens and the Bronx, since these boroughs share the respective counties' names.

Because the five district attorneys are, technically speaking, state officials (since the counties are considered to be arms of the state government), rather than officials of the city government, they are not subject to the [term limitations](/wiki/Term_limit "Term limit") that govern other New York City officials such as the mayor, the [New York City Public Advocate](/wiki/New_York_City_Public_Advocate "New York City Public Advocate"), members of the city council, or the borough presidents. Some [civil court](/wiki/Civil_law_(common_law) "Civil law (common law)") judges also are elected on a borough-wide basis, although they generally are eligible to serve throughout the city.

In some document collections the boroughs used to be designated with a one-letter abbreviation: K for Brooklyn, M for Manhattan, Q for Queens, R for Staten Island (Richmond County), and X for the Bronx.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

## Sixth borough

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Boroughs_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Sixth borough")]

Main article: [Sixth borough](/wiki/Sixth_borough "Sixth borough")

The term "sixth borough" is used to describe any of a number of places that have been [metaphorically](/wiki/Metaphor "Metaphor") called a part of New York City because of their geographic location, demographics (they include large numbers of former New Yorkers), special affiliation, or [cosmopolitan](/wiki/Cosmopolitanism "Cosmopolitanism") character. They have included adjacent cities and counties in the [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area") as well as in other states, [U.S. territories](/wiki/Territories_of_the_United_States "Territories of the United States"), and foreign countries.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) In 2011, New York Mayor [Michael Bloomberg](/wiki/Michael_Bloomberg "Michael Bloomberg") referred to the city's [waterfront](/wiki/Waterfront_(area) "Waterfront (area)") and waterways as a composite sixth borough during presentations of planned rehabilitation projects along the city's [shoreline](/wiki/Shore "Shore"),[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) including [Governor's Island](/wiki/Governor%27s_Island "Governor's Island") in the [Upper New York Bay](/wiki/Upper_New_York_Bay "Upper New York Bay").[[49]](#cite_note-49)

The [Hudson Waterfront](/wiki/Hudson_Waterfront "Hudson Waterfront"), in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), lies opposite Manhattan on the [Hudson River](/wiki/North_River_(Hudson_River) "North River (Hudson River)"), and during the [Dutch colonial era](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland"), it was under the jurisdiction of [New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam") and known as [Bergen](/wiki/Bergen,_New_Netherland "Bergen, New Netherland"). [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey") and [Hoboken](/wiki/Hoboken,_New_Jersey "Hoboken, New Jersey"), in New Jersey's [Hudson County](/wiki/Hudson_County,_New_Jersey "Hudson County, New Jersey"), are sometimes referred to as the sixth borough, given their proximity and connections by [rapid transit PATH trains](/wiki/PATH_(rail_system) "PATH (rail system)") to the city.[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53) [Fort Lee](/wiki/Fort_Lee,_New_Jersey "Fort Lee, New Jersey"), in [Bergen County](/wiki/Bergen_County,_New_Jersey "Bergen County, New Jersey"), opposite Upper Manhattan and connected by the [George Washington Bridge](/wiki/George_Washington_Bridge "George Washington Bridge"), has also been called the sixth borough. [Yonkers](/wiki/Yonkers,_New_York "Yonkers, New York"), in Westchester County, is often referred to as the sixth borough as well.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)
